questions.q: What is the approximate proper (rest-frame) mean lifetime of a muon?
options: \( 2.2\ \mathrm{ms} \)
\( 2.2\ \mu\mathrm{s} \)
\( 2.2\ \mathrm{ns} \)
questions.explanation: A muon at rest lives, on average, about \(2.2\ \mu\mathrm{s}\) (2.2 millionths of a second) before decaying. This is the proper lifetime, measured in the muon's own rest frame where its birth and decay happen at the same place.

questions.q: Travelling at nearly the speed of light, a muon could classically cover at most about 660 m before decaying, yet muons made ~15 km up reach the ground. Without relativity, what fraction should survive the trip?
options: About half of them
Essentially none — a tiny sliver of a fraction of a percent
About 95% of them
All of them, since they move at light speed
questions.explanation: Classically the muons should die more than 14 km short of the ground. The journey is over 20 lifetimes long, and exponential decay leaves an utterly negligible survival fraction. Yet ~10,000 muons per square metre per minute reach sea level, so a classical assumption must be wrong.

questions.q: What are the two postulates of Einstein's special theory of relativity (1905)?
options: Time is absolute; space is relative
The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames; the speed of light in vacuum is the same for all observers
Nothing can travel faster than light; gravity bends spacetime
Mass and energy are equivalent; momentum is always conserved
questions.explanation: Special relativity rests on two postulates: the laws of physics are identical in every inertial (constant-velocity) frame, and the speed of light in vacuum is the same for every observer regardless of motion. The constancy of light's speed forces time and length to be relative.

questions.q: The Lorentz factor is \( \gamma = 1/\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2} \). What happens to \(\gamma\) as the speed \(v\) approaches the speed of light \(c\)?
options: It approaches zero
It stays equal to one
It grows without bound (becomes very large)
It becomes negative
questions.explanation: As \(v \to c\), the term \(v^2/c^2 \to 1\), so \(1 - v^2/c^2 \to 0\) and \(\gamma \to \infty\). At low speeds \(\gamma \approx 1\) and relativistic effects are negligible; near light speed \(\gamma\) blows up, producing dramatic time dilation and length contraction.

questions.q: In Earth's frame, with \(\gamma = 15\), how long does a muon with proper lifetime \(2.2\ \mu\mathrm{s}\) appear to live?
options: \( 0.15\ \mu\mathrm{s} \)
\( 2.2\ \mu\mathrm{s} \)
\( 33\ \mu\mathrm{s} \)
\( 330\ \mu\mathrm{s} \)
questions.explanation: Time dilation gives \(t = \gamma t_0 = 15 \times 2.2\ \mu\mathrm{s} = 33\ \mu\mathrm{s}\) in Earth's frame. The moving muon's internal clock runs slow by a factor of 15, so from our viewpoint it survives 15 times longer — long enough to cross the ~15 km of atmosphere.

questions.q: From the muon's OWN frame, its clock ticks normally and it lives only \(2.2\ \mu\mathrm{s}\). How does it still reach the ground?
options: The atmosphere is length-contracted along the direction of motion, so the muon only has to cross about 1 km
Its lifetime is also dilated in its own frame
It travels faster than light in its own frame
Gravity pulls it down before it can decay
questions.explanation: In the muon's frame it ages normally, but the atmosphere rushes up length-contracted by the same factor \(\gamma\): \(L = L_0/\gamma = 15{,}000/15 = 1000\ \mathrm{m}\). Crossing just ~1 km takes about a lifetime, so it survives. Time dilation (our frame) and length contraction (its frame) give the same outcome.

questions.q: In the Frisch-Smith Mount Washington experiment, ~565 muons/hour were counted at the summit. The classical prediction at sea level was ~27/hour. What was actually measured?
options: About 27 per hour, confirming classical physics
About 409 per hour, matching the relativistic prediction
About 565 per hour, with no decay at all
Zero, all muons had decayed
questions.explanation: They measured 409 ± 9 muons per hour at sea level — close to the relativistic prediction of ~400 and nowhere near the classical ~27. Far more muons survived than classical physics allows, confirming time dilation. The measured dilation factor was 8.8 ± 0.8 against a predicted ~8.4.

questions.q: In the 1971 Hafele-Keating experiment, atomic clocks were flown around the world. What was the key observation on return?
options: All clocks read exactly the same time as the ground clocks
The flown clocks read different times from the ground reference, by amounts matching relativity's predictions
The clocks stopped working in flight
Only the eastbound clock changed; the westbound clock did not
questions.explanation: The eastbound clocks lost ~59 ns and the westbound gained ~273 ns relative to the ground reference, matching the combined special- and general-relativistic predictions. The travelling clocks genuinely experienced different amounts of time — the twin paradox made of caesium and airline seats.

questions.q: GPS satellite clocks are deliberately set to tick slightly slow before launch. Why?
options: To save battery power in orbit
Because once in orbit, relativistic effects make them run fast overall (~38 \(\mu\mathrm{s}\)/day), so pre-setting them slow makes them correct as seen from the ground
Because the satellites are colder in space
To compensate for signal delay through the atmosphere
questions.explanation: In orbit, weaker gravity (general relativity, +45.8 \(\mu\mathrm{s}\)/day) outweighs the motion slowdown (special relativity, -7.2 \(\mu\mathrm{s}\)/day), so the clocks net run fast by ~38 \(\mu\mathrm{s}\)/day. Engineers pre-set the frequency slightly low so the clock ticks correctly once sped up. Uncorrected, position would drift ~10 km/day.

questions.q: A spaceship passes Earth at \(v = 0.6c\). A proper time of 10 s elapses on board (\(\gamma = 1.25\)). How much time elapses in Earth's frame?
options: 8 s
6 s
questions.explanation: Time dilation gives \(t = \gamma t_0 = 1.25 \times 10 = 12.5\ \mathrm{s}\). The moving ship's clock runs slow as seen from Earth, so more time (12.5 s) passes on Earth than the 10 s of proper time aboard the ship. The dilated time is always longer than the proper time.
